India, Pakistan among teams handed direct Asian Games men's cricket knockout berths. [ dawn ]
item.description:
India, Bangladesh, Pakistan and Sri Lanka secured direct entry into the men’s cricket knockout stage at the 2026 Asian Games after the draw was announced on Thursday.

The 10-team men’s competition, starting from September 24 in Nagoya, will begin with a preliminary round featuring Afghanistan, Hong Kong, Japan, Malaysia, Nepal and Oman competing for the remaining quarter-final berths.

Afghanistan, Japan and Nepal were drawn in Group A, while Hong Kong, Malaysia and Oman were placed in Group B. The top two teams from each group will advance to the knockout stage to join the four seeded sides.

The women’s tournament, scheduled from September 17-22, features eight teams and will begin at the quarter-final stage. Defending champions India will face hosts Japan, Bangladesh will take on China, Sri Lanka will meet Malaysia, and Pakistan will play Thailand.

All matches in both competitions will be played in the Twenty20 format.

India swept the cricket gold medals at the 2022 Asian Games, held in 2023 in Hangzhou, claiming both the men’s and women’s titles.

item.title: Brian Lara Stadium hosts maiden Test as West Indies face Pakistan. [ dawn ]
item.description:
West Indies and Pakistan meet at Test cricket’s newest venue in Trinidad on Saturday at the start of a two-match series in which the home side will be enjoying rare favoured status against the visitors.

Brian Lara Stadium, located in the south of the island, has previously hosted one-day and T20 internationals along with regional first-class fixtures.

It becomes the nation’s second Test match venue and 13th in the West Indies, drawing it alongside Queen’s Park Oval in the capital, Port of Spain, the venue of the second Test a week later.

Roston Chase enjoyed his first match and series wins as West Indies Test captain just three weeks ago when the Caribbean team demolished Sri Lanka by an innings and 217 runs and then played out a draw in the second and final Test, both matches played in Antigua.

Chase missed the recent ODI series loss to New Zealand with an injury to his right index finger.

“Roston is a fighter. I know he’s not going to want to miss any Test cricket,” West Indies head coach Daren Sammy said.

“I’m pretty sure Dr. Byam (team physio Denis Byam) has a way of keeping guys on the park, so I’m pretty sure Roston will be ready for the first Test.”

It was their first home Test series triumph since a 2-0 sweep of Bangladesh in 2022, a result which also gave them their last consecutive series wins anywhere as the whitewash of the Bangladeshis was preceded by a notable 1-0 home triumph over England in a three-Test contest.

West Indies are not expected to make significant alterations to the winning combination from the Sri Lankan series, although left-arm spinner Jomel Warrican is likely to come into consideration at the expense of fast-medium bowler Anderson Phillip given the expectation of a pitch favouring spin later in the match.

In-form Ali

In stark contrast, Pakistan have once again revamped their squad in the aftermath of a 2-0 series loss in Bangladesh two months ago with batsman Babar Azam returning as captain in place of Shan Masood in hope of reviving their Test fortunes.

Babar and opening batsman Azan Awais both compiled hundreds in the lone warm-up match against a West Indies Select XI at the weekend while most of the other main batsmen also got confidence-boosting runs.

However the most encouraging aspect of the match in batting-friendly conditions for the tourists was the bowling of pacer Mohammad Ali, who took five wickets in the first innings and will be relied upon, together with the other members of the restructured pace armoury, to give Pakistan a chance to end a miserable run of seven consecutive Test match losses away from home.

Their last visit to the Caribbean in 2021 finished in a 1-1 draw after two Tests in Kingston while the teams’ most recent Test encounters were in the spin-dominant Multan in January of 2025 when the West Indies trumped Pakistan at their own game in the second match to also leave that series at 1-1.

This meeting of two unpredictable sides carries the additional dimension of intervention by the elements as the July/August period is usually one of significant rainfall in the southern Caribbean.

While Trinidad has enjoyed hot, sunny conditions in the days leading up to the first Test, there is certainly no guarantee that such pleasant cricketing weather will be sustained over the fortnight of this short series.

item.title: The need for security. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
Any nation looking to take the next step in its development journey cannot do so without having the defense architecture that keeps pace with the growth.
To that end, we must heed the warning from security and technology experts who have made it clear that Bangladesh’s critical infrastructure is increasingly vulnerable.
In particular, given the exponential expansion of technology and how it is being weaponized for nefarious purposes, beyond simply physical defense, we must urgently strengthen our cyber spaces as well.
Let it be known that this is not a theoretical concern but a real, immediate national security issue, one that Bangladesh can no longer afford to treat as secondary.
Essential systems such as power grids, telecom networks, financial services, ports, and public utilities are all exposed to risks that could disrupt daily life, undermine economic stability, and compromise national sovereignty.
As Bangladesh develops and embraces technology, the threat landscape expands just as quickly, and we must not only recognize this reality, but actively work to address it.
Cyberattacks on critical infrastructure are no longer rare globally but are becoming routine tools of geopolitical pressure, criminal extortion, and sabotage.
Indeed, Bangladesh’s own experience in recent years shows how vulnerable we are. The call for stronger defenses is therefore not merely prudent but necessary.
Protecting critical infrastructure of course is not only about technology but, much like any other issues, equally about governance. Without good governance, even the best tools will fail.
Our future development ambitions depend on stable, secure systems. Be it power, transport, banking, communications, or any other major sector, they all rely on infrastructure that must be defended against increasingly sophisticated threats.
We cannot wait for a crisis to expose its vulnerabilities and must instead act decisively and without delay to ensure these threats remain at bay for the safety and security of our people.

item.title: Better healthcare for a better nation. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The government’s plan to launch door‑to‑door healthcare services is exactly the kind of proactive, people‑centred initiative Bangladesh needs.
For too long, access to healthcare has depended on geography, income, and the ability to navigate overcrowded facilities. Taking essential services directly to citizens - especially the elderly, the poor, and those living with chronic illnesses - is a meaningful step toward equity.
This initiative recognizes a simple truth: Many Bangladeshis do not seek care because they cannot reach it. Distance, transport costs, long queues, and lost wages keep people away from clinics until their conditions worsen. A system that waits for patients to come forward is a system that leaves many behind.
As such, by sending trained health workers into communities, the government is shifting from reactive care to preventive care - and that shift will save lives.
The plan also aligns with Bangladesh’s broader public‑health needs. Non-communicable diseases are rising, and early detection of diabetes, hypertension, respiratory illnesses, and nutritional deficiencies can dramatically reduce long‑term costs and suffering. Door‑to‑door services can therefore bridge these gaps, offering screening, counselling, and referrals before problems escalate.
However, such an initiative only succeeds when it is implemented properly. While Health and Family Welfare Minister Sardar Md Sakhawat Husain spoke of a nationwide program, we hope that it is being piloted properly. Such a program will also be hollow if it fails to reach rural areas, urban slums, and marginalized communities.
It is also evident that Bangladesh needs more initiatives with this level of ambition and outreach. In addition, for healthcare in particular, such a project, while highly commendable, cannot replace community‑based healthcare needing expansion or local clinics strengthened.
Door‑to‑door healthcare can become a cornerstone of a more inclusive, resilient health system for our country, but only if it is scaled, funded, and sustained with seriousness.

item.title: How to solve groundwater depletion. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
Bangladesh’s geography includes the extensive river deltas formed by major rivers like the Ganges and Brahmaputra, alongside a network of hundreds of small rivers. The monsoon season, occurring from June to September, contributes to 70-80% of the annual rainfall, while the dry winter months last from December to February. The annual rainfall distribution varies regionally, with the west receiving around 1,500 mm and the northeast and southeast receiving about 3,000 mm.
Despite being a country with numerous waterways and adequate rainfall, Bangladesh, unusually, faces severe water-related problems in different parts of the country due to low natural recharge and high water demand. The use of surface water has been in decline due to groundwater depletion and dependence on groundwater on the rise. The problem of managing groundwater in the drought-prone northwestern Barind tract is severe, where rainfall is low.
Currently, groundwater is vital for life and livelihoods, serving as the main source of clean drinking water and supporting agriculture. Approximately 90% of groundwater withdrawal is for agricultural use.
In densely populated regions, the demand for groundwater often exceeds the natural recharge rates, leading to a continual decline in water levels, a situation referred to as depletion.
This brings significant challenges, including dry wells, increased pumping costs, and reduced water availability for urban areas and agriculture. The situation intensifies food insecurity and water shortages, particularly as climate change results in more frequent droughts and altered rainfall patterns.
Against this backdrop, addressing groundwater depletion is critical. One strategy for mitigation involves using groundwater within safe yield limits, while another approach includes the artificial replenishment of groundwater aquifers.
In the Barind region, the Barind Integrated Area Development Project officially started in 1985 under the Bangladesh Agricultural Development Corporation. It was created to revitalize the impoverished by combating severe seasonal droughts, severe water scarcity, and the need for food security.
The project was reconstituted and upgraded into the Barind Multipurpose Development Authority in 1992. Groundwater issues were not mitigated and attention was given to the surface water bodies. However, groundwater problems need to be mitigated since 78% of BMDA projects rely on groundwater.
Coastal regions of Bangladesh suffer from severe salinity intrusion, exacerbated by sea-level rise and reduced river flow, rendering water unfit for consumption and agriculture, alongside arsenic and bacterial contamination, leading to health crises.
The managed aquifer recharge (MAR) technique is proposed as a practical solution to these groundwater issues across various regions of Bangladesh. MAR is a strategic approach aimed at intentionally replenishing aquifers with water for subsequent retrieval or enhancement of environmental conditions.
This method supplements natural groundwater recharge by either injecting or infiltrating water into the ground. Various techniques are employed in MAR, including the use of infiltration ponds, injection wells, and riverbank filtration.
Groundwater depletion is a significant issue in the Barind area, where the rate of decline ranges from 0.75 to 1.25 feet per year. This is exacerbated by limited natural aquifer recharge and high irrigation demands, leading to the ineffectiveness of some suction pumps.
To address depletion, two primary strategies are suggested: Utilizing groundwater within safe yield limits, which requires considerable time for aquifer recovery, and enhancing groundwater recharge through artificial recharge mechanisms.
Artificial recharge also offers additional benefits, such as mitigating salinity issues in coastal areas and managing water storage in urban centers like Dhaka. However, the disadvantages of MAR projects should also be taken into consideration before implementation.
Implementing a MAR project necessitates a conceptual understanding of aquifer characteristics, such as the presence and type of top aquitard (clay or silt), aquitard and aquifer thickness, hydraulic conductivity, transmissivity, and specific yield.
Properly designing a recharge well and assigning a recharge rate is critical; designing pumping and recharge wells involve different considerations. A low recharge rate is safer but prolongs the aquifer's recharge time, while a high rate risks clogging the aquifer, which occurs when pores are blocked, preventing adequate water flow if the rate exceeds hydraulic conductivity.
Therefore, careful site specific design of recharge rates and well spacing is essential. The success of a MAR project fully depends on appropriate site specific design of the project.
Water quality is also a significant concern, particularly if contaminated water is used, as it can detrimentally damage aquifers. The preferred water sources rank as follows: 1) rainwater (low contaminated), 2) pond and lake water (moderately contaminated), and 3) river water, which in Bangladesh is compromised by urban pollution.
Studies indicate river water quality varies from fair to marginal, with high levels of sulphate and contamination risks like arsenic and salinity. Thus, river water must be treated to remove contaminants before it can be safely injected into the aquifer.
Dr. Mohammad Abul Fazal is a specialist in agricultural engineering. He achieved his PhD in groundwater management from Kyoto University, Japan.

item.title: US unveils new tariffs on 60 trade partners, including Pakistan, over forced labour concerns. [ dawn ]
item.description:
The United States said on Thursday that it would impose new tariffs on 60 trading partners, including Pakistan, India, China and the European Union, over forced labour concerns, replacing an expiring global duty rolled out by US President Donald Trump earlier this year.

The levies, effective Friday, range from 10 per cent to 12.5pc.

“The United States has had a forced labor import ban for nearly a century, and rigorously enforces it; it’s well past time for our trading partners to do the same,” said US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer.

He earlier added that targeted economies represent the majority of US trade.

The Trump administration has moved swiftly to rebuild the president’s tariff wall after the Supreme Court struck down a host of his duties in February — dealing a blow to his ability to unleash steep levies at will.

After the setback, Trump tapped different authorities to reimpose a 10pc tariff on imports. But this only lasts 150 days, expiring Friday.

The volley of new duties, initially proposed in June, will now take its place.The measures were planned after a months-long investigation and are considered more resistant to legal challenges than earlier moves.

Under the final determination, the US will impose a 10pc duty on goods of Argentina, Bangladesh, Britain, Cambodia, Canada, Ecuador, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, India, Indonesia, Jordan, Malaysia, Mexico, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Trinidad and Tobago.

The European Union, Taiwan, Japan, South Korea and Switzerland were assigned rates that, combined with pre-existing most-favored-nation tariff rates, would total 10pc or 12.5pc.

The other 38 countries, including China, were assigned a 12.5pc rate.

The new levies received swift condemnation from target countries.

Japanese government spokesman Minoru Kihara said Tokyo “regrets” the tariffs, given the higher rate is “solely on the grounds that there is no ban on the import of products produced through forced labour”.

Australia’s trade minister Don Farrell decried the tariffs as “unjustified,” arguing they were “inconsistent with our free trade agreement, and should be removed”.

Goods already facing sector-specific tariffs — like steel and aluminum — will not be impacted.

Certain energy products and fertilisers will be exempt too, alongside goods covered by the US-Mexico-Canada free trade pact, a US official told reporters.

It is worth mentioning that Islamabad had submitted detailed responses to the USTR, including another submission ahead of trade talks earlier this month regarding the forced labour investigations.

Maintaining leverage

Washington is separately investigating 16 economies over excess industrial capacity, in probes that could lead to additional duties. These could result in varying rates among countries eventually, experts warn.

The Trump administration’s move to impose a baseline tariff while sustaining the threat of further duties maintains leverage over its trading partners, trade lawyer Greta Peisch told AFP.

It also creates an incentive for countries to comply with trade pacts that they earlier struck, she added.

In spending time on investigations, officials want their incoming tariffs to be robust if there are court challenges, said Peisch, a former general counsel for the Office of the US Trade Representative, who is now a partner at Wiley Rein.

“This makes it much more likely that they stay for the duration of Trump’s term,” signaling a “much more protectionist world’s largest economy” moving forward, Josh Lipsky of the Atlantic Council told AFP.

Resurrecting tariffs boosts government revenues too, he added.

‘Fragile’ deals

The latest salvo comes shortly after a 25pc tariff took effect on various Brazilian goods, as Washington accused the Latin American giant of unfair trade practices.

This week, Trump also ordered new 50-percent tariffs on many Canadian products, citing Ottawa’s “discriminatory treatment” of American alcohol, automobiles and dairy products.

The Canadian tariffs taking effect in a month rely on an untested legal provision, showing that Trump has other tools to swiftly wield, said Lipsky.

This signals that US tariff deals “are still fragile,” he said.

Nonetheless, the EU, which has signed a trade pact, expects that Washington “will honour the commitments that are spelled out under the EU-US Joint Statement”.

item.title: Turning the tide on child drowning. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
If memory serves me right, I was barely five years old when I narrowly escaped drowning in a village pond. I had not yet learned to swim, but during one of our year-end family vacations, my ego as a city boy got the better of me.
Watching local children dive effortlessly into the water, I felt challenged and foolishly jumped in after them. Fortunately, an older cousin happened to pass by, noticed my struggle, dived into the pond, and pulled me out just in time.
That childhood brush with death has stayed with me ever since. My heart aches whenever I read reports of children losing their lives to drowning, particularly in rural Bangladesh. It is deeply painful to see parents lose their young children in a country crisscrossed by rivers, canals, ponds, and countless natural waterways.
The reality is devastating: Every day in Bangladesh, the equivalent of an entire classroom of children -- around 40 young lives -- is lost to drowning.
Nationally representative data shows that the country records around 18,000 drowning deaths each year, including 11,000 children under the age of five.
About a year and a half ago, the World Health Organization (WHO) released an updated global drowning status report, marking a decade since its landmark Global Status Report on Drowning Prevention in 2014.
At first glance, the findings appeared encouraging for Bangladesh. The report showed that over the previous two decades, the country's drowning death rate had fallen significantly -- from 17 deaths per 100,000 people to six.
When I first read that statistic in isolation, it offered a sense of relief. But that optimism quickly faded after a closer look at the report revealed that Bangladesh's drowning rate remains well above the global average.
According to the WHO, a 38% reduction in the global drowning death rate over the past two decades brought the worldwide average down to 3.8 deaths per 100,000 people in 2024. Bangladesh's rate remains considerably higher -- a public health and child safety challenge that the country can no longer afford to overlook.
Bangladesh deserves recognition for the progress it has made through innovative interventions. However, the data also makes it clear that much more remains to be done, as drowning deaths -- particularly among children -- continue to occur at an unacceptably high rate.
I vividly remember reading a poignant passage in the preface to the 2024 WHO report by Michael R Bloomberg, WHO Global Ambassador for Noncommunicable Diseases and Injuries. He wrote: "Our work began in Bangladesh, where data showed that drowning was the leading cause of death among children aged 1-4. A lack of daytime supervision was putting children at risk, so we established and funded 2,500 community childcare sites. The results were extraordinary -- drowning deaths fell by 88% among daycare-enrolled children, which captured the attention of the national government."
The Bangladesh government later assumed responsibility for funding the program, expanding it to 8,000 community childcare centres that reached around 200,000 children annually across 16 districts. Unfortunately, once the project ended, most of these centres shut down because sustained funding was not available.
It is encouraging that over the past few months, the government has been working on a strategy and financing model to roll out the Integrated Community-Based Centre for Child Care, Protection, and Swim-Safe Facilities (ICBC) project on a much larger scale, covering nearly half of the country's 64 districts.
The initiative is expected to require around Tk800 crore to operate childcare centres across 30 districts. However, concerns remain that administrative delays and bureaucratic inertia could cost more lives in the meantime.
The larger question is why such vital interventions continue to be treated as time-bound projects instead of permanent public services. Drowning is the second leading cause of death among children under five in Bangladesh, after pneumonia. Globally, it is the fourth leading cause of death among children aged one to four and the third leading cause among those aged five to 14.
Why should responsibility for these programs rest solely with the Ministry of Women and Children Affairs? At a time when Bangladesh has significantly increased its health budget -- a welcome departure from years of underinvestment -- why can the government not commit sustained funding to protect children from preventable water-related deaths?
Community-based swimming lessons in both rural and urban areas, alongside well-functioning childcare centres, are investments in children's survival.
In many low-income households, both parents are forced to work, leaving toddlers and young children either unattended or under the supervision of adolescent siblings. As a result, many children drown in ponds, canals and ditches, often only a few metres from their homes.
Studies show that the overwhelming majority of child drownings occur within a short distance of the homestead between 9am and 1pm -- the very hours when rural mothers are busiest with household or agricultural work.
When the ICBC centres were fully operational, Bangladesh recorded a noticeable decline in drowning incidents in the program areas. The closure of those daycare centres has created a dangerous gap, forcing children back into inadequate supervision that reflects the economic realities facing many low-income families.
The 2024 WHO report warns that more than 7.2 million people, most of them children, could die from drowning worldwide by 2050 if current trends continue. Yet the vast majority of these deaths are preventable through proven community-based interventions.
The WHO recommends installing barriers to limit children's access to water, providing safe childcare facilities for preschool-aged children, and teaching school-aged children basic swimming, water safety, and safe rescue skills.
It also advocates training community members in resuscitation, raising public awareness, enforcing boating and shipping safety regulations, and strengthening flood risk management.
If the government considers a permanent financial commitment too costly, it should explore partnerships with local communities, charities, corporate social responsibility (CSR) initiatives and the private sector to ensure these childcare centres remain operational.
Bangladesh has yet to embrace the concept of community hubs that combine childcare, swimming instruction, sports facilities and arts and cultural activities within local neighbourhoods.
Local government institutions, elected representatives, business leaders, community organizations, and youth volunteers could all play a meaningful role in sustaining such initiatives -- provided the government establishes a clear policy framework and long-term institutional support.
As countries prepare to observe World Drowning Prevention Day this Saturday (July 25), Bangladesh needs to intensify measures to save children from drowning.
Declared by the United Nations General Assembly in April 2021, this global advocacy event highlights the tragic impact of drowning on families and communities while promoting life-saving solutions.
The global theme for World Drowning Prevention Day 2026 is "Unite to turn the tide," urging all sectors of society to work together toward the goal of reducing global drowning deaths by 35% by the year 2035.
Over 90% of drowning deaths occur in low- and middle-income nations, frequently in rivers, lakes, domestic water containers, and open wells.
To address this crisis effectively, Bangladesh must vigorously implement the following actions:
Reaz Ahmad is Editor, Dhaka Tribune.

item.title: Two decades of mass protests in India. [ dawn ]
item.description:
Thousands of supporters of India’s youth-led “cockroach” movement have been protesting since June, demanding the education minister resign over exam paper leaks, in the biggest challenge to Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his third term.

Activists say the leaks showed widespread corruption, a complaint which has brought crowds onto the streets for years. Following are details of major rallies, triggered by graft and other crimes and causes, over the past two decades.

April 2011

Anti-corruption activist Anna Hazare started the first of two hunger strikes in a bid to push the government to bring in anti-graft legislation.

Hundreds of thousands rallied to his support across the country and he was briefly jailed by the then-Congress alliance government.

India’s parliament eventually passed an anti-graft law, though Hazare said it watered down his demands. Some of his supporters went on to form the Aam Aadmi Party in 2012, now one of Modi’s opponents in parliament.

December 2012

Public fury over the gang-rape of a young woman on a moving bus in New Delhi erupted into mass protests in the capital.

Crowds blockaded roads, broke through police lines in street confrontations and called for an overhaul of a system and a society they said had failed to tackle violence against women. Many demanded the culprits be executed.

The 23-year-old woman died in a Singapore hospital a few days after the attack. In response, India amended laws to expand the definition of sexual assault and set up fast-track courts to speed up trials in such cases.

December 2019

India’s parliament passed a new citizenship law that brought hundreds of thousands of people to the streets in protests and triggered bouts of sectarian violence that killed at least 76 people.

The Citizenship Amendment Act aimed to fast-track citizenship for Hindus, Parsis, Sikhs, Buddhists, Jains and Christians who arrived in India on or before December 31, 2014 from neighbouring Muslim-majority Afghanistan, Bangladesh and Pakistan.

Critics said the law, alongside a proposed national register of citizens, discriminated against Muslims and violated the spirit of India’s secular constitution. Some feared the government would remove the citizenship of Muslims without documents in some border states.

India implemented the law in March 2024.

September 2020

Thousands of farmers blocked roads and railway tracks in protests against new legislation that they say could pave the way for the government to stop buying grain at guaranteed prices, leaving them at the mercy of private buyers.

Modi’s government defended the bills, saying they would help rid India’s vast agriculture sector of antiquated laws and allow farmers to sell to institutional buyers and big retailers.

The protests lasted more than a year before Modi repealed the laws.

June 2022

An overhaul of military recruitment, designed to bring in fitter, younger troops and reduce pension expenditure, triggered rail blockades and protests across the country.

Recruits, veterans and opposition leaders objected to the introduction of fixed four-year contracts, saying people would struggle to get other jobs once their service was over.

Modi’s government refused to roll back the programme but offered instead to reserve 10 per cent of state vacancies for those who had served the four years.

August 2024

Thousands of doctors took to the streets for months after the rape and murder of a trainee doctor in a government hospital in the eastern city of Kolkata.

Protesters demanded safer workplaces, a crackdown on violence against women and a swift investigation into the attack.

item.title: The Karim Khan saga: a timeline. [ middleeasteye ]
item.description:
The Karim Khan saga: a timeline

Submitted by Sondos Asem on Thu, 07/23/2026 - 14:09
How allegations against the ICC’s chief prosecutor became entangled with pressure over his pursuit of Israeli officials, culminating in a vote that could reshape the court
Chief Prosecutor Karim Khan at the International Criminal Court in The Hague on 14 March 2025 (AFP/file photo)
Off

Member states of the International Criminal Court (ICC) are due to vote at the United Nations' headquarters in New York on Friday on whether to remove the court's chief prosecutor, Karim Khan, from office – a first in the court’s 24-year history. 

The vote caps a saga stretching back more than two years, spanning sexual misconduct allegations Khan has consistently denied, a UN-led investigation, a judicial panel that unanimously cleared him, and a bureau that chose to override that finding and press ahead regardless.

The case has unfolded alongside a separate campaign of pressure on Khan and the court over his pursuit of war crimes warrants against Israeli and Hamas leaders, from a threat by Britain's then foreign secretary to defund the ICC, to sanctions imposed on Khan and dozens of ICC officials by the Trump administration, to the US secretary of state's public vow to "dismantle" the court.

Khan, a British barrister, is the ICC's third chief prosecutor since the court’s founding in 2002. 

In addition to seeking warrants for Israeli leaders, his office has sought arrest warrants for Russia’s Vladimir Putin, the Philippines' Rodrigo Duterte, Myanmar’s junta leaders, and Taliban officials in Afghanistan. 

Middle East Eye has been at the forefront of exclusive coverage of the misconduct case at all stages.

Below is a timeline of how the case unfolded, from early 2024 till the highly controversial 24 July vote.

Palestine investigation, and the pressure campaign begins (2021–2023)

February 2021 Karim Khan is elected ICC chief prosecutor by the Assembly of States Parties (ASP). 

March 2021 Khan's predecessor, Fatou Bensouda, opens the ICC's investigation into alleged war crimes in the occupied Palestinian territories.

21 June 2021 Khan takes the oath of office, becoming the third person to hold the post since the court's founding in 2002.

Summer 2022 The woman who later becomes the complainant in the sexual misconduct case alleges a separate senior ICC official behaved inappropriately towards her.

31 August 2022 Khan writes to the ASP president raising her concerns about that other official; external investigators are appointed in October 2022 with her cooperation.

11 December 2023 That separate external investigation concludes there was "no unsatisfactory conduct" by the official concerned.

17 November 2023 Five states (South Africa, Bangladesh, Bolivia, Comoros, Djibouti) formally refer the Palestine situation to the prosecutor after the 7 October Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent Israeli war on Gaza.

December 2023 Khan visits Israel (kibbutzim, the Nova festival site) and meets Palestinian officials including President Mahmoud Abbas in Ramallah. 

Lead-up to the warrants (January–April 2024)

January 2024 Khan convenes an independent, seven-member expert panel to assess whether the legal threshold for arrest warrants for Israel's Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, his then Defense Minister Yoav Gallant, and three Hamas leaders had been met. The panel unanimously concludes in March the process was "fair, rigorous and independent" and sufficient material existed to request warrants.

16 March 2024 Khan's legal team concludes it will be in a position to apply for the Israeli and Hamas warrants by the end of April.

21 March 2024 Khan orders a "complementarity" check into whether Israel was investigating the alleged crimes itself; his office concludes it was not.

ICC issues arrest warrants for Israel's Benjamin Netanyahu and Yoav Gallant
Read More »

25 March 2024 Khan informs the US administration of his decision and that warrants would be sought by the end of April.

15 April 2024 In London, Khan tells UK Justice Secretary Alex Chalk he will apply for the warrants; he had wanted to brief Foreign Secretary David Cameron directly, but Cameron was abroad.

19 April 2024 A senior US official warns Khan by phone of "disastrous consequences" if he proceeds; Khan says he rejected this, citing Israel's lack of cooperation.

23 April 2024 While Khan is on an official visit to Venezuela, Cameron phones him and says issuing warrants for Netanyahu and Gallant would be "like dropping a hydrogen bomb". Cameron spoke aggressively, told Khan he would "lose the dressing room" and be seen as "losing the plot", and said the UK would "defund the court and withdraw from the Rome Statute" if warrants were issued. Khan replied that this would mean "the rules-based system would be dead".

In an account of the episode in MEE journalist Peter Oborne's book, Complicit: Britain’s Role in the Destruction of Gaza, a source close to Cameron said that the call with Khan did take place and was “robust”. But the source said that rather than making a threat, Cameron pointed out that strong voices in the Conservative Party would push for defunding the ICC and withdrawing from the Rome Statute.

24 April 2024 12 Republican US senators (including Marco Rubio) send Khan a letter warning "Target Israel and we will target you." 

25 April 2024 British journalist Douglas Murray writes in the New York Post that Khan will seek warrants within days, before this is public knowledge.

26 April 2024 Netanyahu publicly criticises the ICC on social media, saying: “Under my leadership, Israel will never accept any attempt by the ICC to undermine its inherent right of self-defence.”

28 April 2024 Israel says it will allow Khan to visit Israel and Gaza, an unprecedented offer that delays the warrant applications.

29 April 2024 A colleague of Khan's raises harassment allegations against him in a private conversation.

30 April–1 May 2024 Khan describes meetings with western states and US officials that he characterises as efforts to dissuade him from proceeding.

Warrants filed, allegations surface (May 2024)

2 May 2024 The sexual misconduct/harassment allegations are first put to Khan in person by members of his team, on the same day, per his later ICC submission, that he had been planning to announce the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants. Khan separately meets Dutch counterterrorism officials over a security briefing about suspected Mossad activity in The Hague.

Exclusive: How Karim Khan’s Israel war crimes probe was derailed by threats, leaks and sex claims
Read More »

3 May 2024 A colleague of Khan refers the harassment allegations to the ICC's Internal Oversight Mechanism (IOM).

7 May 2024 The IOM's first inquiry closes after the complainant declines to cooperate.

19–20 May 2024 With Israel's promised permission letter for the Gaza visit still not delivered, Khan cancels the trip and, on 20 May, announces he is seeking arrest warrants for Netanyahu, Gallant, and Hamas leaders Yahya Sinwar, Ismail Haniyeh and Mohammed Deif. In his public statement he says: "I insist that all attempts to impede, intimidate or improperly influence the officials of this Court must cease immediately."

21 May 2024 David Cameron publicly calls the warrant decision "a mistake", accusing the ICC of drawing a "moral equivalence" between Israeli and Hamas leaders.

10 June 2024 The outgoing Conservative government files a formal objection disputing ICC jurisdiction over Israeli nationals; the incoming Labour government drops the objection the following month, after the July 2024 general election.

Leaks, a second complaint, arrest warrants (October–November 2024)

17 October 2024 An anonymous Twitter account begins circulating details of the harassment allegations against Khan.

19 October 2024 The Mail on Sunday reports that ASP President Paivi Kaukoranta has confirmed the allegations exist; an official ASP statement follows later on 24 October.

October–November 2024 A second IOM investigation opens and closes after the complainant again declines to cooperate. 

11 November 2024 The ASP president subsequently decides to seek an outsourced UN investigation into Khan via the Office of Internal Oversight Services (OIOS).

21 November 2024 ICC Pre-Trial Chamber I unanimously approves and issues the arrest warrants for Netanyahu and Gallant, the first time the court has targeted officials of a western-allied state. It also rejected two Israeli challenges to the case under Articles 19 and 18 of the Rome Statute.

Sanctions and threats (February–August 2025)

6 February 2025 Trump issues Executive Order 14203 imposing sanctions on ICC officials, with Khan as the initial target, over the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants; his US visa is revoked, his wife and children are barred from the US, and UK bank accounts are frozen.

19 May 2025 Khan takes voluntary leave pending the OIOS investigation; deputy prosecutors Nazhat Shameem Khan and Mame Mandiaye Niang take over the leadership of his office. 

May 2025 Shortly before going on leave, Khan had been preparing new warrant applications for Israeli ministers Bezalel Smotrich and Itamar Ben Gvir over West Bank settlement expansion.

‘Financial death penalty’: How US sanctions are upending the lives of ICC judges
Read More »

5 June 2025 US Secretary of State Marco Rubio announces sanctions against four ICC judges: Beti Hohler (Slovenia) and Reine Alapini-Gansou (Benin) for their role in the Netanyahu/Gallant arrest warrants; and Luz del Carmen Ibanez Carranza (Peru) and Solomy Balungi Bossa (Uganda) for authorising the Afghanistan investigation. 

9 July 2026 The US sanctions UN Special Rapporteur on Palestine Francesca Albanese over her cooperation with the ICC. 

16 July 2025 ICC judges reject an Israeli request to withdraw the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants pending Israel's jurisdictional appeal.

20 August 2025 US imposes sanctions on judges Kimberly Prost (Canada) and Nicolas Yann Guillou (France), plus deputy prosecutors Nazhat Shameem Khan (Fiji) and Mame Mandiaye Niang (Senegal).

4 September 2025 US sanctions three Palestinian NGOs for cooperating with the ICC. 

13 October 2025 MEE reveals that Margareta Kassangana, a Polish diplomat and ASP vice president, met privately with Khan's accuser to discuss the case before the Bureau of the Assembly of States Parties, the court's govening body, referred the matter to the UN's OIOS in late 2024.

MEE also reports on a recorded summer 2025 phone call in which another bureau member, Uganda's ambassador Mirjam Blaak, is heard telling a colleague she believed Khan "may not come back", describing the complainant in personal terms, and repeating Netanyahu's since-debunked claim that Khan rushed the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants to pre-empt a "honey trap" defence. 

Disqualification bid and secret Smotrich filing (November 2025–May 2026)

17 November 2025 Israel formally asks the ICC to disqualify Khan from the Palestine investigation and withdraw the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants, citing the misconduct allegations as evidence of bias. The court has yet to rule on the request.

3 December 2025 The ASP's annual assembly in The Hague issues a declaration resisting US demands to drop the Palestine/Afghanistan investigations and amend the Rome Statute, without naming the US directly.

Exclusive: ICC prosecutor's office seeks arrest warrant for Israel's Smotrich
Read More »

10 December 2025 Khan submits a statement to the ICC's appeal chamber responding to Israel's disqualification request, describing for the first time a "senior UK government official" (confirmed by MEE as Cameron) who threatened UK funding withdrawal.

11 December 2025 The OIOS delivers its finalised report (150 pages, with roughly 5,000 pages of supporting evidence) to the ASP-appointed judicial panel for a legal determination.

18 December 2025 The US sanctions judges Gocha Lordkipanidze (Georgia) and Erdenebalsuren Damdin (Mongolia) after their rejection of Israel's appeal against the Gaza investigation.

22 January 2026 Responding to a Freedom of Information request, the UK Foreign Office confirms for the first time that the Cameron–Khan call took place on 23 April 2024.

9 March 2026 MEE breaks the story that a three-judge panel (Leona Theron of South Africa, Paul Lemmens of Belgium and Seymour Panton of Jamaica) unanimously concludes the OIOS findings "do not establish misconduct or breach of duty" by Khan. Lemmens, in a separate opinion, says he has "serious doubts" the allegations could ever meet the required standard, but joins the unanimous conclusion. 

23 March 2026 MEE reports that some bureau member states seek to sabotage the judges' panel's report. 

2 April 2026 A majority of the bureau votes to disregard the panel's conclusion and make its own assessment of the evidence. MEE names the 15 states that voted this way as Belgium, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Cyprus, Ecuador, Finland, Italy, Japan, Latvia, New Zealand, Poland, Slovenia, South Korea and Switzerland.

2 April 2026 The prosecutor's office secretly files an arrest warrant application for Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich, alleging war crimes, crimes against humanity, and apartheid over West Bank settlement policy. It was the same warrant prepared by Khan before he went on leave but, as MEE reported, it was shelved for about a year.

Khan speaks out publicly for first time (April–May 2026)

29 April 2026 Khan gives his first public comments since going on leave, in an interview with journalist Mehdi Hasan on the outlet Zeteo. He says he thought the judges' panel finding "was the end of the matter" and is "perplexed" the bureau didn't close the case immediately. He calls the theory that he rushed the Netanyahu/Gallant warrants to distract from the allegations "baloney", noting that he told the US and others he would seek warrants in March 2024, before any allegation was raised. He says Russian and Israeli intelligence had been conducting "close surveillance" of him. 

8 May 2026 In an interview with MEE’s David Hearst Podcast, Khan accuses bureau members of running a "dangerous", biased campaign against him and says the disciplinary process is a "Khan-made process... specific to me".

Bureau's decision, run-up to vote (June–July 2026)

8 June 2026 The bureau votes to suspend Khan and, by a two-thirds majority, finds he committed "serious misconduct". MEE reports the vote as 14 in favour (Belgium, Brazil, Chile, Cyprus, Ecuador, Finland, Italy, Japan, Latvia, New Zealand, Poland, Slovenia, South Korea, Switzerland), four against (Kenya, Senegal, Sierra Leone, Uganda), three abstaining (Bolivia, Bosnia, South Africa).

The confidential decision, seen by MEE, frames the finding as Khan having "engaged in a sexual relationship" with the complainant that was improper given the power imbalance, a notable shift from the original non-consensual-conduct allegation. Khan denies any sexual relationship; the complainant's account centres on non-consensual conduct. Khan's lawyers say this reframed allegation was never put to him.

18 June 2026 MEE reveals the bureau has set 24 July, at UN headquarters in New York, as the date and venue for the full ASP's special session.

13 July 2026 US Secretary of State Marco Rubio explicitly threatens that Washington would "dismantle the ICC – brick by brick, if necessary".

16 July 2026 CNN airs an interview with Khan’s accuser going public for the first time, standing by her allegations of non-consensual sex. 

17 July 2026 ASP President Kaukoranta writes to member states confirming the bureau failed to reach the required majority on two separate retaliation allegations against Khan (from other staff members), which are now closed as unproven, distinct from the sexual misconduct finding still going to the full Assembly.

18 July 2026 Josep Borrell, the former EU foreign policy chief, writes that the bureau's process amounts to a "political vote" serving what he calls a US-Israeli "offensive" to "defang" the court, citing Rubio's stated intent to dismantle the ICC "brick by brick".

21 July 2026 Khan's legal team issues an open letter to ASP members stating that formal channels for Khan to make representations have been closed, that the ASP president discouraged states from meeting with his team, and that Khan and his lawyers have been denied accreditation to attend the 24 July session.

24 July 2026 (scheduled) The full ASP votes by secret ballot at UN headquarters, New York, under Article 46 of the Rome Statute, on the single combined motion covering both the misconduct finding and Khan's removal.

International law

item.title: Kashmir, IWT among key global challenges threatening 'peaceful coexistence', Dar tells Asean moot. [ dawn ]
item.description:
Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar on Thursday drew attention to key challenges threatening peaceful coexistence and stability across the world, listing the Kashmir dispute and India’s threats to Pakistan’s water share among them.

He made the remarks at the 33rd ministerial meeting of the Asean Regional Forum (ARF) held in Manila, the Philippines.

Foreign ministers and delegates from 27 member states of ARF gathered for the summit, which comes amid a flare-up in the war between the United States and Iran. The conflict is a key concern for the 11-nation Asean, which has a combined gross domestic product of about $3.8 trillion heavily reliant on Middle East oil.

Addressing the meeting, DPM Dar highlighted eight key challenges that he said “continue to threaten peaceful coexistence, stability and our livelihoods across the region and the world”.

“Any delay in addressing these challenges will only undermine our collective efforts to achieve peace, stability and prosperity across our regions and beyond,” he said.

Noting the “continued failure” to resolve longstanding disputes in South Asia, he particularly called attention to the Jammu and Kashmir dispute as “the heart of regional instability”.

He said that Pakistan continued to advocate for a “just and peaceful” settlement, in line with the United Nations Charter and resolutions as well as the aspirations of the Kashmiri people.

The deputy premier also expressed concern once again over India’s decision to unilaterally place the Indus Water Treaty (IWT) in abeyance after hostilities flared up between the two nations last year.

“We simply cannot afford to let this serious provocative development remain unaddressed,” Dar said, urging the entire international community to support a peaceful resolution to the issue.

The issue of water has remained a bitter point of contention between the two nuclear-armed nations since May 2025. Earlier this month, the top brass of Pakistan’s army vowed to undertake “all measures necessary” to ensure the availability of Pakistan’s rightful share of water.

US-Iran war and Gaza

Dar also highlighted Pakistan’s role in facilitating a ceasefire between the US and Iran and convening talks at the leadership level between the two arch-enemies after 47 years.

Appreciating the confidence placed in the country by the US, Iran, Gulf countries and others, he said, “These hard-earned achievements through dialogue and diplomacy have created a valuable opportunity to advance lasting peace. We must not let this opportunity slip through our hands.”

He expressed his hope that Pakistan’s efforts to bring peace would contribute to regional stability, as well as the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and the resumption of normal shipping. He called on the international community to reinforce these efforts through sustained dialogue, diplomacy and constructive engagement.

Thirdly, the deputy premier called attention to the ongoing humanitarian crisis in occupied Palestinian territories, calling it “deeply alarming”.

“Our collective failure to prevent the genocidal streak unleashed in Gaza raises serious questions about our collective conscience as human beings, and about our ability to prevent the continued atrocities in the region,” he said.

FM Dar reiterated Pakistan’s support for Palestinian self-determination and the establishment of an independent, sovereign, viable and contiguous state of Palestine based on pre-1967 borders.

‘Common threat’ of terrorism

DPM Dar also highlighted the issue of terrorism, terming it “a common threat and challenge for all of us in varying degrees”.

“Pakistan has been at the forefront of the global fight against terrorism. No country outside active conflict zones has been sacrificing as [much as] Pakistan,” he said, highlighting that over 90,000 lives had been lost as well as close to $150 billion in economic damages.

Despite all odds, he said, the country was determined to fight against all forms of terrorism. He stressed that this long-term commitment required sustained national efforts and enhanced international cooperation.

In particular, he pointed out the challenge of terrorist groups operating from Afghan soil, naming the Tehreek-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) and the Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) as groups exploiting Afghan territory to launch attacks against Pakistan.

“Pakistan has only one expectation for the Afghan Taliban regime: to not allow Afghan territory to be used for terrorism against Pakistan or any other neighbouring country,” DPM Dar said.

“If they are able to honour these commitments and fulfill their repeated promises, there will be immense opportunity for trade, connectivity and socio-economic development across South Asia, Central Asia and beyond.”

The deputy premier called on the Afghan Taliban regime and abetters of regional terrorism to refrain from allowing Afghan territory to be used in this regard, as well as urging ARF members to support these efforts.

There has been a resurgence in terrorism in Pakistan since the Afghan Taliban returned to power in Kabul in 2021. Islamabad has repeatedly urged the Taliban administration to dismantle terrorist sanctuaries on Afghan soil, particularly those linked to the banned TTP. Officials say those appeals have gone unheeded.

Ukraine war, South China Sea tensions

On the Ukraine-Russia war, Dar expressed the country’s support for diplomatic efforts aimed at a “just and lasting peace”, saying that dialogue and diplomacy were the only path forward.

He also addressed a recent incident in the South China Sea, saying that Pakistan valued its ties with all Asia-Pacific states and urging “non-interference, restraint and engagement”.

Tension is currently simmering between Beijing and close US ally the Philippines over an incident in the South China Sea that led each to summon the other’s ambassador.

Beijing claims sovereignty over almost the entire South China Sea via a line on its maps that cuts into the exclusive economic zones of Brunei, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam, and is at the centre of long-running disputes over a multitude of islands and features.

The deputy prime minister also reaffirmed Pakistan’s commitment to the One-China principle recognising the People’s Republic of China as the “sole legitimate authority” over Taiwan. China claims Taiwan as its own territory, which Taiwan rejects.

Climate change

Finally, the minister addressed the issue of climate change, noting that Pakistan remained among the countries most vulnerable to its effects despite being responsible for less than one per cent of emissions.

He highlighted the country’s “ambitious” climate agenda and called for enhanced international cooperation to address what he termed a “common global challenge”.

The deputy premier called on the forum to strengthen its preventive diplomacy role by remaining “dynamic, forward-looking and focused on practical action”.

Dar welcomed the adoption of the Manila Plan of Action 2026-2036 and reaffirmed Pakistan’s commitment to its implementation as well as the ARF’s principles and objectives.

“As we move forward, let us renew our collective resolve to build a peaceful, inclusive and resilient region where sovereign equality, mutual respect and dialogue remain a foundation of our shared future,” he concluded.

Meetings with other FMs

On the sidelines of the ministerial meeting today, the deputy premier also held bilateral meetings with his counterparts from several participating ARF member states.

These included Oman’s FM Badr Albusaidi, New Zealand’s FM Winston Peters, Australia’s FM Penny Wong, and the United Kingdom’s newly appointed foreign secretary Ed Miliband.

Dar arrived in Manila on Wednesday. He met with the foreign ministers of Russia, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, as well as Asean Secretary-General Dr Kao Kim Hourn and the European Union’s top diplomat Kaja Kallas.

He also attended a dinner meeting hosted by Egypt’s FM Dr Abdelatty, where the two ministers held wide-ranging discussions.

The DPM visited the headquarters of the Asian Development Bank (ADB), where he met with the bank’s Vice President (South, Central and West Asia) Yingming Yang, Vice President (Sectors and Themes) Fatima Yasmin, and Executive Director for Pakistan Nashita Mohsin.

“Established in 1994, the ARF has remained a pivotal platform for multilateral consultations on regional security issues. Since becoming a member in 2004, Pakistan has maintained an active role within the forum, consistently offering constructive contributions to its regional programmes and objectives,” the FO noted on Tuesday.

item.title: Andy Burnham should put policy first. [ dhakatribune ]
Taking our ties with the Maldives further. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The Maldives' growing interest in recruiting more skilled Bangladeshi professionals while broadening bilateral cooperation beyond traditional labour migration is certainly a promising development for both countries.
For decades, Bangladeshi workers have played an indispensable role in supporting the Maldivian economy, particularly in construction and hospitality.
Now, as the island nation expands its healthcare, tourism, and infrastructure sectors, its demand for nurses, accountants, and other skilled professionals presents Bangladesh with a valuable opportunity to elevate the nature of this partnership.
This prospect, however, must be approached strategically if it is to deliver maximum mutual benefit.
Bangladesh cannot simply view this as another overseas employment destination. Rather, it should treat it as an incentive to invest more heavily in technical education, vocational training, professional certification, and language skills.
Producing a workforce that consistently meets international standards will not only strengthen Bangladesh's reputation abroad but also enable workers to command better wages and enjoy improved career prospects.
The opportunities extend well beyond labour exports: Discussions on greater cooperation in trade, tourism, renewable energy, maritime connectivity, and climate diplomacy point to a partnership with significant long-term potential.
A direct shipping route, if realized, could reduce transport costs and facilitate greater commercial exchange, while collaboration on climate resilience is particularly relevant given the shared vulnerabilities of both low-lying nations.
The responsibility now lies with policy-makers in both countries to translate these encouraging discussions into tangible outcomes.
Bangladesh must strengthen skills development, improve worker protection, and ensure ethical recruitment practices, while the Maldives should continue creating transparent pathways for skilled migrants and expanding avenues for broader economic cooperation.
This evolving relationship represents an opportunity to build a mature, diversified partnership that benefits both economies. With careful planning, sustained diplomatic engagement, and a commitment to shared growth, Bangladesh and the Maldives can turn this promising prospect into a lasting success.

item.title: The push for export diversification. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The government's ambition to achieve double-digit export growth in promising sectors such as leather, agro-processing, and light engineering is an encouraging sign for Bangladesh's economy, but only if it translates into reality.
At a time when the country is preparing for its post-LDC future, broadening the export base is no longer merely desirable -- it is essential.
The Commerce Ministry's emphasis on supporting these sectors and creating a more business-friendly environment reflects an understanding that Bangladesh must reduce its overwhelming dependence on ready-made garments if it is to sustain long-term economic growth.
The garment industry has been the backbone of Bangladesh's export success for decades, but relying too heavily on a single sector has exposed our economy to global market fluctuations, shifting trade policies, and changing consumer demand. Diversification is, therefore, a safeguard against future uncertainty.
Fortunately, Bangladesh possesses considerable untapped potential. Leather and leather goods, pharmaceuticals, agro-processing, information technology, light engineering, shipbuilding, and jute-based products all have the capacity to become significant export earners.
However, unlocking that potential requires sustained investment in infrastructure, uninterrupted energy supplies, simplified regulations, easier access to finance, and stronger support for innovation and research.
Particular attention should also be paid to small and medium-sized enterprises and emerging entrepreneurs. These businesses often struggle to enter international markets due to limited resources and inadequate institutional support.
By facilitating participation in international trade fairs, strengthening export promotion initiatives, and helping businesses comply with global quality standards, the government can nurture the next generation of exporters.
Ultimately, export diversification should be viewed as a national priority rather than a short-term policy objective. A broader export portfolio will strengthen economic resilience, create higher-value employment, and enhance Bangladesh's competitiveness in the global marketplace.
If backed by consistent reforms and effective implementation, the government's vision of export growth can become far more than an aspiration -- it can serve as the foundation for a stronger and more sustainable economy.

item.title: Climate action can save 88,000 lives a year. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The latest estimate that air pollution contributes to nearly 88,000 premature deaths every year in Dhaka should stop us in our tracks. Behind this staggering number are parents who never return home, children whose lungs never fully develop, older people whose lives are cut short, and families burdened by illness that was largely preventable.
We often discuss air pollution and climate change as two separate crises. In reality, they are two symptoms of the same problem.
The overwhelming majority of greenhouse gases and harmful air pollutants originate from the same sources: Burning fossil fuels in power plants, industries, brick kilns, transport systems, and households.
Every litre of diesel burned, every ton of coal consumed, and every inefficient brick kiln operating releases carbon dioxide that warms the planet alongside fine particulate matter and toxic gases that damage human health.
This means that every climate action is also an air quality intervention.
When we replace fossil fuels with renewable energy, improve public transport, electrify vehicles, increase energy efficiency, modernize industries, or phase out polluting brick kilns, we are not only reducing Bangladesh's contribution to climate change but allowing people to breathe cleaner air almost immediately.
Scientists often describe this as a "co-benefit" of climate action. But perhaps that term understates its significance. Saving tens of thousands of lives every year is not merely an added benefit; it should be one of the primary reasons for accelerating climate action.
Too often, climate mitigation is portrayed as an expensive obligation undertaken for future generations or to meet international commitments under the Paris Agreement. That framing misses the enormous domestic benefits.
Cleaner air means fewer heart attacks and strokes. It means fewer cases of asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer, diabetes, and complications during pregnancy. It means healthier children, more productive workers, lower healthcare costs, and stronger economic growth.
In other words, climate policy is public health policy.
Yet our institutions often continue to treat these issues separately.
Air pollution is addressed through environmental regulation and public health measures, while climate change is discussed in the context of international agreements, emissions targets, and energy transitions. Different ministries, different budgets, different strategies, but the same sources of pollution.
This separation risks missing one of the greatest opportunities available to Bangladesh.
Imagine if every transport investment, every industrial policy, every energy project, and every urban development plan were evaluated not only for its contribution to economic growth and carbon reduction, but also for the number of lives it could save through cleaner air.
Climate and clean-air policies should not compete for attention or funding -- they should reinforce one another through a single integrated strategy.
Bangladesh has already demonstrated remarkable leadership in climate adaptation. From cyclone preparedness to community-based resilience, the country has shown that it can pioneer innovative responses to climate risks.
The next chapter of leadership should be to integrate climate mitigation and clean-air policies into one coherent national agenda -- not because international negotiators demand it, but because Bangladeshi citizens deserve clean air.
This shift is becoming increasingly important.
Bangladesh is urbanizing rapidly, electricity demand continues to grow, transport systems are expanding, and industrial production is increasing.
Decisions made today about energy infrastructure, mobility, urban planning, and manufacturing will determine both our emissions trajectory and the quality of the air millions breathe every day.
Fortunately, many of the solutions are already known.
Expanding renewable energy reduces dependence on fossil fuels. Investing in reliable and affordable public transport reduces congestion and vehicle emissions. Accelerating electric mobility, improving industrial efficiency, modernizing brick production, protecting urban green spaces, and enforcing air quality regulations all deliver immediate health gains while contributing to long-term climate goals.
These are not competing priorities. They are mutually reinforcing investments in Bangladesh's future.
International experience consistently shows that the economic value of improved health often offsets a substantial share of the cost of climate mitigation. Fewer hospital admissions, lower medical expenditures, reduced absenteeism, and higher labour productivity generate benefits that are rarely captured in traditional climate debates.
Perhaps it is time to change the conversation.
Instead of asking: "Can Bangladesh afford climate action?" we should ask: "Can Bangladesh afford the human and economic cost of treating climate change and air pollution as separate challenges?"
Every year of delay means thousands of avoidable deaths, millions of sick days, and billions of taka in lost productivity and healthcare costs.
The estimate of 88,000 lives lost annually should become more than another alarming statistic. It should become a catalyst for a new way of governing -- one that recognizes that reducing greenhouse gas emissions and cleaning the air are not parallel ambitions, but two outcomes of the same decisions.
Bangladesh does not have to choose between economic development and climate action. The smartest development pathway is also the healthiest one.
By accelerating the transition towards cleaner energy, cleaner transport, cleaner industries, and more sustainable cities, the country can simultaneously reduce greenhouse gas emissions, improve competitiveness, and protect the health of millions.
The air we breathe and the climate we inherit are inseparable. Our policies should be as well.
If Bangladesh succeeds in bringing climate and clean-air policies together under one integrated vision, it will not only help slow global warming. It could save tens of thousands of Bangladeshi lives every year.
That is perhaps the strongest argument for climate action of all.
Dr Avit Bhowmik is a Climate Scientist and Associate Professor of Risk and Environmental Studies, Karlstad University, Sweden.

item.title: Cockroach Janta Party protest: How liberal scepticism induces paralysis in oppositional politics. [ dhakatribune ]
item.description:
The most corrosive feature of India’s current political landscape is not merely the overweening power of the ruling dispensation, but the fundamental collapse of the capacity to distinguish truth from fiction among those who seek to oppose it.
In a climate where truth is already a precarious commodity, a new and more insidious pathology has taken hold: A habit of suspicion that sees every stirring of civic life not as a sign of democratic vitality, but as a meticulously choreographed puppet show.
The discussions over the past few weeks about the emergence of the satirical Cockroach Janta Party and the hunger strike by Ladakhi activist Sonam Wangchuk provide a clinical study in this self-inflicted paralysis.
Rather than engaging with the substance of these mobilizations, a significant section of liberal opinion-making has retreated into a hall of mirrors.
By framing these movements as “staged” or as “false flag” operations engineered by the state, the Opposition’s intellectual vanguard is committing a double error. It is alienating potential allies and, more tragically, it is undermining the very possibility of political action.
The tragedy of contemporary Indian liberalism is its transition from a project of institutional defence to a project of conspiratorial narcissism. There is a seductive comfort in believing that everything is a ruse. It relieves the commentator of the hard work of organizing and it protects the ego from the messiness of unpredictable political outcomes.
If every protest is a plant, then the failure to mobilize need not be explained.
But this “narrative suspicion” has costs. In any healthy democracy, spontaneous civic dissent is a bridge that provides the raw material political parties must refine into institutional pressure.
When influential voices preemptively dismiss these movements as “engineered,” they do the ruling party’s work for them. They create a vacuum where there should be an alliance. They signal to the public that no one -- not even the ascetic or the aggrieved student -- can be trusted.
This does more than just critique the state: It inadvertently validates the state’s claim that all dissent is manufactured and illegitimate.
Political judgement is being turned into a personal performance for social media platforms. Driven by the incentives of the attention economy, pundits now prioritize the “sharp take” over the patient gathering of evidence.
There is a status-seeking dimension to this scepticism. To be the one who sees through the charade offers a sense of intellectual superiority that a more cautious, evidence-based approach lacks.
Yet, this is a hollow victory. While the commentator gains followers, the political movement loses its footing.
The consequence is a “demobilization.” Opposition parties, already hesitant and strategically adrift, are further spooked by these whispers of sabotage.
They recoil from engagement, fearing they might be the dupes of a grander malign scheme.
This fragmentation of the anti-establishment discourse ensures that civic energy, rather than being channeled into a coherent challenge to power, is dissipated in a series of internecine squabbles over authenticity.
If Opposition politics is to regain any semblance of traction, it must first recover a sense of democratic faith.
This does not mean a naive acceptance of every claim. Scepticism is a vital democratic virtue. But scepticism must be a tool for uncovering truth, not a shield for avoiding the responsibility of political engagement.
Responsible critics must move away from the sensationalist shortcut and toward a more demanding standard of verification.
They must learn to distinguish between the presence of suspicious actors (who exist in every movement) and the underlying legitimacy of the grievance.
To treat every protest as a tactical feint by the regime is to grant the regime a degree of omnipotence it does not possess.
Democracy thrives on the unpredictability of the street and the courage of the individual actor. By drowning these in a sea of conspiracy, the liberal intelligentsia is not protecting the republic -- it is ensuring its own irrelevance.
The task of the critic is not to unmask every movement until nothing remains, but to provide the intellectual architecture that allows genuine dissent to stand.
Without that, the only thing being eaten by conspiracy is the possibility of a democratic future.
Milind Murugkar writes on economic and political issues. This article first appeared on Scroll.in and is being republished with permission.

item.title: Shifting Winds: Bangladesh in an Age of Fragmented Interdependence. [ tbsnews ]
Dar arrives in Manila to attend Asean Regional Forum ministerial meeting. [ dawn ]
item.description:
Deputy Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar arrived in Manila on Wednesday to participate in the 33rd Asean Regional Forum (ARF) ministerial meeting, the Foreign Office (FO) said.

“A busy programme of engagements lies ahead, including participation in the ARF plenary meeting and bilateral meetings with counterparts on the sidelines,” the FO said in a post on the social media platform X.

It said that upon his arrival, Dar was received by Elmer G Cato, the assistant secretary at the Philippines’ foreign affairs department, Pakistan’s Ambassador to the Philippines Asima Rabbani and other officials.

In a statement issued on Tuesday night, the FO said that the high-level meeting will bring together foreign ministers and delegates from 27 ARF member states, adding that the Asean secretary general will also attend the meeting.

It said that Dar will participate in discussions on “political and security issues affecting the Asia-Pacific region, with a special focus on promoting peace, stability, and mutual cooperation through sustained dialogue and diplomacy”.

He will also hold bilateral meetings with his counterpart from the Philippines and several other participating dignitaries on the sidelines of the meeting, it said.

“Established in 1994, the ARF has remained a pivotal platform for multilateral consultations on regional security issues. Since becoming a member in 2004, Pakistan has maintained an active role within the forum, consistently offering constructive contributions to its regional programmes and objectives,” the FO statement concluded.

Dar meets with Russian, Bangladesh FMs

On the sidelines of the Asean meeting, Dar met with Bangladesh FM Khalilur Rehman, who is also the president-elect of the 81st session of the United Nations General Assembly, the FO said.

The ministers “reviewed the positive trajectory of Pakistan–Bangladesh relations and the renewed impetus by the deputy prime minister’s visit to Bangladesh last year”, the statement said, adding they reaffirmed their “shared commitment to further strengthening bilateral ties”.

The FO further said that the two dignitaries welcomed growing engagement between their countries, including “enhanced people-to-people contacts and improved connectivity following the commencement of direct flights this year”.

They agreed to sustain the “positive trajectory”, the statement added. Dar and Rehman also exchanged views on “regional and international issues of mutual interest”, the FO said.

In January, direct flights between Pakistan and Bangladesh commenced after a 14-year hiatus as an inaugural flight from Dhaka landed at Karachi Airport.

Ahead of the Asean moot, Dar met with Russian FM Sergey Lavrov in Manila.

The two leaders reviewed bilateral relations between their countries and reaffirmed their commitment to “further strengthening cooperation in trade, energy, connectivity, and other key areas, as well as enhancing coordination at multilateral forums”, the FO said.

“They underscored the importance of sustained high-level engagement in advancing the Pakistan-Russia partnership,” it added.

The two foreign ministers also exchanged views on key regional and international developments and agreed to remain in close contact.

Dar visits ADB headquarters in Manila

On the sidelines of his visit to Manila, Dar also visited the headquarters of the Asian Development Bank (ADB), where he was received by the Director General for the Central and West Asia Department, Leah Gutierrez, the FO said.

Dar signed the ADB Visitors’ Book upon his arrival. The FO said “the interaction provided an opportunity to exchange views on the ADB’s evolving development agenda, its partnership with Pakistan, and the role of Pakistani professionals at the institution”.

According to the FO, the discussions focused on the bank’s development agenda and “the important role being played by Pakistani professionals in advancing the Bank’s work across a broad range of sectors and regions.”

Dar appreciated their contributions to the ADB’s mission and acknowledged the distinction they bring to Pakistan through their leadership and expertise, it added.

He also congratulated Nashita Mohsin on assuming her responsibilities as Pakistan’s executive director to the ADB and wished her success in advancing Pakistan’s engagement with the bank.
